The weekly promotional schedule data is still important to capture, because those features can improve forecast accuracy when retraining occurs. By downloading the promotions weekly, you ensure that the model always has the most recent feature data available by the time the monthly retraining job runs.

Using the Scheduler API to orchestrate the monthly retraining makes the process reliable and automated. The Scheduler API can trigger a Vertex AI pipeline or custom retraining job on a fixed cadence. This setup balances cost control with data freshness.

If you wait until the end of the month to download promotions as well, you lose valuable weekly detail that should be incorporated into the next training run.

Sales figures only refresh monthly, so retraining every week wastes compute cycles without meaningful model improvement.

Relying solely on Vertex AI Model Monitoring to detect drift in the promotions data doesn’t guarantee the model will be retrained promptly once new sales data is available. You risk having stale models until drift is severe enough to trigger.

question:
You are building an ML model for a global restaurant review platform. Your goal is to predict review volume based on restaurant type in different urban areas. The training dataset includes binned latitude and longitude to represent location, and one-hot encoded restaurant types. How should you construct your features to best model the relationship between restaurant type and review volume in each area?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Use three separate features: binned latitude, binned longitude, and one-hot encoded restaurant type
options.B: Use the product of raw latitude, raw longitude, and restaurant type
options.C: Combine binned latitude, binned longitude, and one-hot encoded restaurant type into a single feature cross
options.D: Create two separate feature crosses: one for binned latitude and restaurant type, and one for binned longitude and restaurant type

explanation:
The best choice is to create one combined feature that crosses binned latitude, binned longitude, and restaurant type. This produces a single identifier for each city-zone and restaurant type pairing, helping the model distinguish spatially specific patterns.

Using individual features does not encode the interaction between location and type. Multiplying raw coordinates with restaurant types produces values with no clear meaning. Making two partial crosses splits the spatial context and does not allow the model to see full geographic combinations. A single cross over all three features better captures localized variation in review volume.

question:
Your team manages a machine learning pipeline that forecasts passenger demand for a public transportation network. The preprocessing step runs on Google Cloud Serverless for Apache Spark using PySpark to aggregate and clean ridership data from multiple sources. The training step uses a Vertex AI custom training job in Python. Right now, after the preprocessing job completes, the training job has to be started manually. You need to design a fully automated and maintainable solution that links both steps, enforces dependencies, and provides execution tracking. What should you do?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Write a Python script that calls the Google Cloud Serverless for Apache Spark API for preprocessing, waits for completion, then submits the custom training job to Vertex AI using the REST API. Schedule the script on Cloud Scheduler to run daily
options.B: Use the Kubeflow Pipelines SDK to define a Google Cloud Serverless for Apache Spark component for preprocessing and a custom training component, then run them in a Vertex AI Pipelines job
options.C: Use a Vertex AI Workbench notebook to call the Google Cloud Serverless for Apache Spark preprocessing job, export the output to Cloud Storage, then load it manually into the training step. Run all steps sequentially in the notebook cells
options.D: Create a Dataflow job that performs preprocessing and, upon completion, sends a Pub/Sub message that triggers a Cloud Run Function to start the training job in Vertex AI

explanation:
The correct answer uses Kubeflow Pipelines on Vertex AI to connect Google Cloud Serverless for Apache Spark preprocessing and Vertex AI training in one orchestrated workflow. The pipeline enforces the order of execution, keeps the steps modular, and provides tracking of pipeline runs. This approach integrates both stages without manual intervention and avoids rewriting the training code while ensuring scalability and maintainability.

The other answers fall short in different ways. The Python script with Cloud Scheduler can automate execution but lacks built-in pipeline management, lineage tracking, and integration with Vertex AI Pipelines. The Vertex AI Workbench notebook depends on manual runs and is not well suited for production automation. The Dataflow and Pub/Sub method can trigger training but is not an ML pipeline solution and lacks the cohesive tracking, reproducibility, and dependency enforcement provided by Kubeflow Pipelines.

question:
You are building a churn prediction model for a subscription streaming service. Your dataset includes a column for subscription type, which has several possible categories. You split the data into training and validation sets, then apply one-hot encoding to the subscription type column in the training data. You notice that one subscription type present in the training data is missing from the validation data. What should you do?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Use label encoding for the validation data instead of one-hot encoding
options.B: Apply one-hot encoding to the categorical variables in the validation data using the same categories from the training set
options.C: Merge the missing category with the most similar category in the validation data
options.D: Remove the subscription type column entirely from the validation data

explanation:
The correct approach is to ensure that the validation data is encoded with the same category mapping as the training data, even if a category does not appear in the validation set. This keeps the feature space consistent and prevents the model from misinterpreting the inputs. A category missing in the validation data should still have its column present with values set to zero. This is a standard step in preprocessing pipelines to ensure compatibility between training and validation phases.

The other options are incorrect because label encoding changes the feature representation and can distort relationships for models that expect one-hot encoded inputs. Merging categories based on similarity changes the problem definition and could introduce bias. Removing the entire feature from the validation data breaks alignment with the training set, leading to inconsistent input dimensions and invalid evaluation results.

question:
You are working as a machine learning engineer for a streaming service. The product team wants to improve the recommendation engine so that users are shown new shows or movies based on their watch history and preferences of users with similar behavior. You are asked to design a model to support this initiative.

What type of model should you build?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Collaborative filtering model
options.B: Regression model
options.C: Rule-based system
options.D: Knowledge-based filtering model

explanation:
Collaborative filtering is well suited for recommending items based on past user behavior and similarities across users. It does not require detailed metadata about the content itself. Instead, it focuses on patterns in what users have interacted with and what similar users have liked or watched.

A regression model could predict ratings, but it would rely heavily on labeled data and content features, and it would not fully capture user-to-user similarity. A knowledge-based filtering model helps when recommendations are based on explicit content relationships, not patterns in user behavior. Rule-based systems rely on heuristics or popularity but do not personalize results based on individual watch history.

question:
You work for a logistics company that tracks thousands of delivery vehicles in real time. Your team has trained a TensorFlow model that predicts the likelihood of a delivery being late based on current GPS data, driver history, and weather conditions. Incoming location data is sent to Pub/Sub, and a Dataflow streaming job processes these messages, writes predictions to BigQuery, and sends alerts for high-risk deliveries. You need to integrate your model into the pipeline while keeping end-to-end latency as low as possible for immediate alerting. What should you do?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Deploy the model to a Vertex AI prediction endpoint and make requests from the Dataflow pipeline
options.B: Host the model using TensorFlow Serving on Google Kubernetes Engine and call it from the Dataflow job
options.C: Package the TensorFlow model within the Dataflow job so it can perform inference in-process without external service calls
options.D: Containerize the model and run it on Cloud Run, invoked by Dataflow during processing

explanation:
The correct approach is to integrate the trained TensorFlow model directly into the Dataflow job so that predictions are executed locally in the same process as the pipeline steps. This avoids network round trips to external services and keeps latency to a minimum, which is crucial when working with real-time streams that drive immediate actions such as alerting dispatch teams. Since the model is preloaded as a dependency, the pipeline can handle large volumes of messages with consistent and predictable inference times.

The other options all introduce external calls that add latency and potential points of failure. Calling a Vertex AI endpoint or a TensorFlow Serving instance on GKE means each prediction involves network overhead, which can be unpredictable under load. Using Cloud Run for inference also requires an HTTP call and could be subject to cold starts, further delaying results. These approaches are more suitable when you need to decouple model hosting from the processing pipeline, but they do not align with the strict low-latency requirement described in this scenario.

question:
A healthcare analytics company is building a custom training pipeline on Vertex AI to predict patient readmission risk. The training data is relatively small and stored in Cloud Storage. The training code uses scikit-learn and several other nonstandard Python packages that are not included in the default prebuilt containers. The data science team wants to minimize the startup time for each training job while making sure they can easily rerun the job multiple times during development. How should they configure the custom training job?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Use the scikit-learn prebuilt container, download the training data from Cloud Storage at runtime, and install the required dependencies using pip within the training script
options.B: Build a custom container image that contains both the training code and the dataset, then load the dataset from within the container when the job starts
options.C: Build a custom container that has all required dependencies pre-installed, store the dataset in Cloud Storage, and read it into the training job from Cloud Storage at runtime
options.D: Use the scikit-learn prebuilt container, include the dataset in the container, and install the dependencies as a step in the training application before model training begins

explanation:
The correct solution is to create a custom container with all dependencies already included and store the dataset in Cloud Storage. This keeps the container lightweight while avoiding runtime dependency installation, which can significantly delay startup. Reading a small dataset from Cloud Storage has negligible impact on performance and allows data updates without rebuilding the container. This approach minimizes startup time, simplifies iteration, and keeps both data and code management flexible.

The other options introduce unnecessary delays or complexity. Installing dependencies at runtime slows job startup and can make results inconsistent if package versions change. Embedding the dataset inside the container increases image size, making it slower to pull and less flexible to update data without rebuilding the image. Using a prebuilt container without modification forces you to install dependencies during execution, which is slower than having them pre-installed. Storing data in Cloud Storage and pulling it at runtime is faster and more scalable than bundling it directly into the container for this scenario.

question:
Your company is building a mobile platform for a large gym chain that supports millions of users. You’ve developed a model that predicts whether users will miss their next scheduled workout based on patterns in app activity and location data. You want to send users a push notification if the model predicts that they are likely to skip.

How should you serve your predictions and send notifications to users?

Choose only ONE best answer.

options.A: Create an individual Pub/Sub topic for every user. Deploy a Cloud Run service that sends a notification when the model forecasts the user will likely skip a workout that day
options.B: Create a separate Pub/Sub topic per user. Use an App Engine service that scans model outputs and sends reminders if a user is predicted to miss their scheduled workout
options.C: Use Firebase Cloud Messaging to deliver notifications. Register each user by ID in Firebase, and send an alert when the system-wide average likelihood of skipped workouts crosses a threshold
options.D: Use Firebase Cloud Messaging to deliver personalized notifications. Register each user by ID in Firebase, and trigger an alert when the model predicts they are likely to skip a workout

explanation:
The best solution is to use Firebase Cloud Messaging with individual user IDs in a Firebase instance. This setup lets you send direct, real-time alerts to each user when the model predicts they are likely to miss their workout. FCM is built for scalable, targeted messaging to both mobile and web apps.

Creating a Pub/Sub topic per user is not scalable and adds operational overhead. Using App Engine increases complexity without adding targeting advantages. Sending alerts based on system-wide averages doesn't meet the requirement of user-level prediction and notification.
